author: Dr. Manuel Reyes
content:
URGENT CARE VISIT NOTE — 1/12/2026, 10:45 AM
Patient: Sandoval, Maria Isabel (MRN: MRN-90215734)
DOB: 8/12/2003 (Age 22)
Pay status: Self-pay; sliding-scale arrangement initiated at registration.
Interpreter: Spanish-language phone interpreter used throughout visit (Language Line interpreter #44218).

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
"Mis articulaciones me duelen mucho y se me esta cayendo el cabello" — "My joints hurt a lot and my hair is falling out."

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Sandoval is a 22-year-old Spanish-speaking woman with no significant past medical history who presents today with approximately 6 months of progressive bilateral joint pain, swelling, and morning stiffness, with associated fatigue, malaise, and diffuse hair thinning. She reports onset around August 2025 with intermittent aching in her wrists and finger joints, which she initially attributed to her work in housekeeping. Over the subsequent months, the joint discomfort has become more persistent, symmetric, and now involves multiple small joints of the hands, wrists, and both ankles. She reports morning stiffness lasting approximately 1 hour, which improves with movement.

In the past 2-3 months she has additionally noticed increasing fatigue, diffuse scalp hair thinning that she finds in her hairbrush and on her pillow, occasional night sweats, and unintentional weight loss (estimated several pounds, no scale at home). She denies fever, rash, oral or genital ulcers, photosensitivity, Raynaud phenomenon, dysphagia, dyspnea, chest pain, or sicca symptoms. No bowel changes or blood in stool. She denies eye redness or vision change. No recent travel, no tick exposures, no new pets, no known sick contacts. Last menstrual period approximately 5 weeks ago — reports menses have become irregular over the past 4 months.

She has tried over-the-counter ibuprofen 400mg several times daily with mild and inconsistent relief. She has not seen a clinician since 2019 — she was followed at Johns Hopkins Community Health briefly after immigration but lost Medicaid coverage in 2020 and has been uninsured since. She has not been able to access a primary care provider.

She came to urgent care today because she could not get a fork to her mouth easily this morning due to wrist pain and stiffness, and her mother insisted.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Positive for fatigue, malaise, night sweats, mild unintentional weight loss; denies fever, rigors.
- HEENT: Positive for diffuse hair thinning. Denies oral ulcers, gum bleeding, eye dryness, eye pain.
- Cardiopulmonary: Denies chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations.
- GI: Denies nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, dysphagia, diarrhea, melena.
- GU/Reproductive: Irregular menses over 4 months; not sexually active currently; denies dysuria, hematuria.
- Musculoskeletal: Bilateral hand, wrist, and ankle pain; morning stiffness >1 hour. Denies back pain, hip pain. No prior trauma.
- Skin: Hair thinning. Denies rash, photosensitivity, ulcers, alopecia patches.
- Neuro: Denies headache, focal weakness, numbness.
- Psych: Denies depression or anxiety; reports occasional sadness about being uninsured.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
Iron deficiency anemia (2018, resolved with iron supplementation).
Latent TB ruled out at immigration entry visit (2018).
Otherwise denies medical problems.

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY: None.

MEDICATIONS: OTC ibuprofen 400mg PRN; multivitamin intermittently.

ALLERGIES: NKDA.

FAMILY HISTORY: Maternal aunt with SLE (diagnosed in Guatemala in 20s, disabled by chronic illness). Mother with hypothyroidism and pre-diabetes. Father deceased age 39 in MVA (had treated TB in 2010).

SOCIAL HISTORY: Immigrated from rural Guatemala in 2017 at age 14. Lives with mother, younger brother, and aunt in East Baltimore. Works ~25-30 hours/week in hotel housekeeping; no insurance. Denies tobacco, alcohol, drug use. Not sexually active currently. Last travel: Guatemala 2019.

VITAL SIGNS:
T 98.4 F (36.9 C), HR 92, BP 116/72, RR 16, SpO2 99% RA. Weight 50.8 kg (112 lb). Height 150 cm (4'11"). BMI 22.6. Pain 5/10 (joints).

PHYSICAL EXAM:
General: Young woman, appears stated age, well-nourished, in no acute distress. Speaks with mother present. Cooperative through interpreter.
HEENT: Normocephalic. Scalp shows diffuse thinning of hair, no patchy alopecia, no scaling or erythema. Conjunctivae pink, non-injected. Oropharynx clear, no ulcers. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Thyroid not enlarged, no nodules.
CV: RRR, normal S1/S2, no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, rales, or rhonchi.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly. No bruits.
MSK: Symmetric synovitis of bilateral MCPs (2nd-5th) and PIPs (2nd-4th) with tenderness, mild warmth, and decreased range of motion. Wrists tender to palpation with limited dorsiflexion bilaterally. Ankles mildly swollen with tenderness over the talocrural joints bilaterally. No deformities. Grip strength reduced bilaterally due to pain. Squeeze test of MCPs and MTPs positive.
Skin: No rash. Diffuse scalp hair thinning as noted. No nail pitting or onycholysis. No livedo.
Neuro: Cranial nerves II-XII intact grossly. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups (when not limited by joint pain). Sensation intact. DTRs 2+ throughout.

LABS PERFORMED TODAY (point-of-care + send-out):
- CBC: WBC 4.75, Hgb 12.6, Plt 273
- Basic Metabolic Panel: Na 138, K 4.0, Cl 103, CO2 24, BUN 11, Cr 0.4, Glucose 88
- UA dipstick: pH 6.0, no protein, no blood, no leukocytes, no nitrites
- TSH: 5.32 (reference 0.30-4.00) — slightly elevated
- ANA: positive at 1:80 titer, homogeneous pattern
- Rheumatoid factor: <10 (negative)
- ESR: pending (send-out)
- CRP: pending (send-out)

IMPRESSION / PLAN:
New presentation of subacute polyarthritis with constitutional symptoms. No established diagnosis.

1. Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg PO BID — 60-day sample/cash-pay supply provided. Counseled re: ocular toxicity, need for baseline ophthalmology.
2. Levothyroxine 25 mcg PO daily, fasting. Recheck TSH in 6-8 weeks.
3. Continue OTC ibuprofen PRN; counseled re: GI/renal risks.
4. Referrals: rheumatology (provided list of sliding-scale clinics), ophthalmology (HCQ baseline), primary care (Esperanza Health FQHC).
5. Additional labs in next 2 weeks: CMP, dsDNA, anti-Smith, anti-Ro/La, C3/C4, anti-CCP, hepatitis B/C, HIV, free T4, TPO Ab, UA + protein:creatinine ratio, parvovirus B19 IgM/IgG.
6. Return precautions reviewed (Spanish written): new rash, fever, chest pain, dyspnea, dark/foamy urine, eye pain, severe abdominal pain.
7. RTC 2 weeks if no specialty follow-up secured.

Visit time: 65 min total (interpreter time 32 min).
attestation:
Visit documented in real time. Patient and mother given written discharge instructions in Spanish.

Electronically signed by: Dr. Manuel Reyes, MD
Date: 1/12/2026
